title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption:
The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita, 2023

The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national income and health outcomes for countries in 2023, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against the burden of disease on the vertical axis. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time, expressed as Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people. Circles sized by population distinguish large nations from smaller ones, while a color-coded legend identifies regions including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a clear negative relationship: countries with lower GDP per capita consistently occupy the upper-left quadrant with high disease burdens, while wealthier nations cluster in the lower-right with low disease burdens. The Central African Republic sits at the extreme high end of the burden scale, exceeding 80,000 DALYs per 100,000 people, alongside Niger, Chad, and Burundi which all register above 60,000. In contrast, Singapore, Japan, and Germany appear at the far right with GDP per capita figures approaching or exceeding $50,000 and disease burdens falling below 20,000 DALYs. Large Asian populations like India and China occupy the middle ground, with disease burdens roughly between 20,000 and 40,000 DALYs and GDP per capita ranging from $5,000 to $20,000. Greenland and the United States appear as outliers with high income but slightly elevated disease burdens compared to their European peers. The distribution forms a distinct curve where increases in GDP per capita coincide with steep declines in DALYs at lower income levels, flattening out as nations reach higher income thresholds.
